front: Identify the most likely diagnosis on this image.

back: Most likely diagnosis: carotid-cavernous fistula (CCF). Key clue: dilation of the superior ophthalmic vein.

front: Identify the most likely diagnosis on this image.
back: Most likely diagnosis: hemangiopericytoma (solitary fibrous tumor spectrum).

front: Differentiate the skull base lesion shown from its main imaging mimic.
back: Clival chordoma is typically midline, lytic on CT, and heterogeneously enhancing. Main differential: chondrosarcoma, which is usually eccentric/petroclival, often with chondroid matrix calcification, and may appear bright on ADC.

front: Identify the most likely diagnosis at the jugular foramen.
Identify the persistent embryonic carotid-basilar anastomosis pattern.
back: Most common persistent anastomosis: persistent trigeminal artery (from the cavernous ICA). Rare variants include the otic artery (petrous ICA), hypoglossal artery (cervical ICA through the hypoglossal canal), and proatlantal artery (cervical ICA/ECA).

front: Identify the tumor type and key arterial feeder.
back: Tentorial meningioma. A classic feeder is the artery of Bernasconi and Cassinari (meningohypophyseal trunk).
Hemangiopericytoma/solitary fibrous tumor pattern: staghorn vessels, typically CD34 positive and EMA negative.
Chordoma: cords/sheets of physaliphorous (vacuolated) cells in a myxoid matrix; typically positive for cytokeratin, EMA, S100, and brachyury.
Chondrosarcoma: S100 positive and EMA negative, with malignant cartilaginous differentiation.

front: What structures pass through the jugular foramen?
back: Jugular foramen contents include CN IX (glossopharyngeal), CN X (vagus), CN XI (accessory), the jugular bulb/internal jugular vein, the inferior petrosal sinus, and meningeal arterial branches.

front: What structures pass through the foramen rotundum?
What structures pass through the foramen ovale?
back: Primary structure: mandibular nerve (CN V3). Additional transmitted structures can include the lesser petrosal nerve, accessory meningeal artery, and emissary veins.

front: What are the contents and borders of the anteromedial triangle?
back: Anteromedial triangle: medial border CN V1, lateral border CN V2, base between the superior orbital fissure and foramen rotundum; contents include the sphenoid sinus and CN VI in relevant exposures.

front: What are the contents and borders of the anterolateral triangle?
back: Anterolateral triangle: medial border CN V2, lateral border CN V3, base between foramen rotundum and foramen ovale; no consistent named neurovascular content.

Gradenigo syndrome is a complication of petrous apicitis classically presenting with the triad of persistent otorrhea, retro-orbital/facial pain in the CN V distribution, and diplopia from CN VI palsy.

Tolosa-Hunt syndrome is idiopathic granulomatous inflammation of the cavernous sinus/superior orbital fissure causing painful ophthalmoplegia with multiple cranial neuropathies; it is typically steroid responsive.

front: What are ocular manifestations of CCF?
back: Ocular findings in carotid-cavernous fistula include conjunctival injection/chemosis, proptosis, cranial neuropathy-related diplopia/ophthalmoplegia, elevated intraocular pressure, and possible vision decline.
